title: The Bachelor Party

jokes.text: David's t-shirt is missing the 'with' — implying it reads 'Bachelor Party' instead of 'Bachelor Party with Patrick'
jokes.setup: David tells Stevie he didn't want a 'jokey' bachelor party, implying he had standards for the event
jokes.punchline: He immediately complains his t-shirt is missing the word 'with' — revealing he had an absurdly specific vision and the shirt is already a petty disaster
jokes.explanation: Classic David Rose character comedy — his obsessive specificity about the shirt is perfectly in character. The joke works because his complaint reveals both his pretension and his inability to just enjoy things. Strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: This shirt says 'David's Bachelor Party.' There's no apostrophe before the S.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: It's possessive.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's incorrect. It should be 'David's' with an apostrophe. How is this supposed to be fun if it's grammatically wrong?

jokes.text: Stevie: 'Now, before I tell you what I have planned for tonight's festivities, I think we can all agree that putting me in charge of your joint bachelor party was a big mistake, right?'
jokes.setup: Stevie is about to reveal the party plans she spent time organizing
jokes.punchline: She preemptively and cheerfully throws herself under the bus before anyone can complain, defusing all expectation
jokes.explanation: Pure Stevie deadpan. The joke is that she says it cheerfully and proactively, before anyone complains. Her self-awareness is the joke and it's well-structured — it also creates comic tension for everything that follows.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: This shirt has a grammatical error. Who approved this?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I did. Intentionally.

jokes.text: David sent Stevie a seven-page e-mail with the subject line 'The Bachelor Party of My Dreams'
jokes.setup: David and Patrick insist they 'never said' they wanted it to be the bachelor party of their dreams
jokes.punchline: Stevie reveals David sent a literal seven-page email with that exact subject line
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The specificity of 'seven pages' and the formal subject line perfectly encapsulates David's delusional self-importance and denial. It's deeply character-integrated — only David would do this and then deny having expectations. The structure is clean: denial → devastating receipt. Quotable and rewatchable.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I'm going to fail at this. I know I'm going to fail at this. But here are the plans.

jokes.text: David: 'Yes. Just depends on what she's planned.' — followed by his reaction seeing the plan
jokes.setup: Patrick tells David to say they'll be happy with whatever Stevie planned
jokes.punchline: David immediately qualifies his agreement with 'just depends on what she's planned' — then his visible dismay at the reveal undercuts his false enthusiasm
jokes.explanation: Solid David Rose irony — his inability to commit to even performative enthusiasm is the joke. The qualifier 'just depends on what she's planned' is wonderfully David. A reliable laugh but not an extraordinary one.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I have never once asked Patrick for anything specific.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: That's not true. You sent me a seventeen-page email about your morning routine.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That was not a request, that was a courtesy.

jokes.text: David: 'Isn't that a Jodie Foster movie?' — confusing an escape room with Panic Room
jokes.setup: Stevie announces they'll be doing Elmdale's finest escape room as part of Patrick's bachelor party wish
jokes.punchline: David immediately conflates 'escape room' with the film 'Panic Room,' revealing his cultural knowledge is all pop-media and zero practical experience
jokes.explanation: This is well-executed character comedy. David's frame of reference being entirely cinematic (and wrong) is very in-character. It also seeds a running thread that pays off when Moira later references 'Panic Room' correctly. Solid and earns a rewatch bonus for the callback setup.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Those aren't appetizers, those are errors.

jokes.text: David: 'You lost me at fun group game.'
jokes.setup: Stevie explains that an escape room is a fun group game where you solve puzzles
jokes.punchline: David's flat rejection of the entire premise at the word 'fun group game' — his social anxiety and preference for solitary intellectual pursuits expressed in five words
jokes.explanation: Maximum economy. Five words that capture David's entire character. The joke works because the audience has learned enough about David to feel the truth of it immediately. The 'you lost me at X' structure is familiar but the specific phrase 'fun group game' earns it.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: We're doing an escape room experience.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not interested in being locked in a room while someone tries to kill me.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: It's not like Panic Room, David. You solve puzzles and clues to get out.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So it's exactly like Panic Room, except without Jodie Foster.

jokes.text: David: 'I'm sorry, my family agreed to this?'
jokes.setup: After learning about the escape room, David is incredulous his family would agree to a group activity
jokes.punchline: His shock isn't at the escape room — it's that his family voluntarily agreed to do something together, implying they're all individually anti-social
jokes.explanation: Decent family-dynamics joke that works because we know the Roses. A reliable chuckle, but not particularly sharp on its own.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: It's like a fun group activity. You get locked in a room with your friends and you have to solve puzzles to escape.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Why would I want to be locked in a room with my friends?

jokes.text: Stevie: 'Your family hasn't responded to a single email, so they will be doing what we tell them to do.'
jokes.setup: David asks if his family agreed to the escape room
jokes.punchline: Stevie reveals the family's participation isn't consent — it's coercion by default due to their collective failure to respond to emails
jokes.explanation: Sharp and efficient. It also tracks perfectly with Moira's voicemail joke later where she tries to get out of it. The idea that the whole family ignores emails is both a family-wide character gag and a plot-logic joke.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Did my family agree to this?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Well, no one said no.

jokes.text: David: 'Is there anything else on my list that we could do this evening?' — implying he sent a wishlist alongside the seven-page email
jokes.setup: The seven-page 'Bachelor Party of My Dreams' email has already been established
jokes.punchline: David casually references 'his list' — implying the seven-page email had an accompanying list of activities, deepening the absurdity of his demands
jokes.explanation: A good escalation of the email joke. The fact that there's also a separate list (beyond the seven-page email) escalates the absurdity without over-explaining it.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, a woman of my stature requires certain accoutrements to maintain her sense of self-worth. A diamond tennis bracelet isn't merely a piece of jewelry—it's a fundamental necessity, like oxygen, or a well-appointed powder room. Without it, I'm simply a woman in a dress, and that won't do at all.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'If you could come up with the money for the Tahitian dolphin cruise, I'd happily change plans.'
jokes.setup: David asks if there's anything else on his list they could do
jokes.punchline: Stevie reveals one of David's actual requests was a Tahitian dolphin cruise — the specificity is absurd but completely believable for David
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'Tahitian dolphin cruise' is what makes this. It's not just 'an expensive trip' — it's the exact caliber of absurd luxury that David would genuinely request. High character integration and a great button to the email running gag.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I had opportunities, David. Opportunities that I let slip through my fingers like sand through an hourglass, or a sieve, or... some other permeable object.

jokes.text: Moira explains she demanded a job 'for a hundred grand more than Clifton, and a diamond tennis bracelet' — and when asked what the bracelet was for, she says 'Dramatic effect. Symbolism.'
jokes.setup: Moira tells the story of negotiating for a prestigious acting job
jokes.punchline: The diamond tennis bracelet demand was purely theatrical — she admits it was for 'dramatic effect' and 'symbolism,' not actual desire for the bracelet
jokes.explanation: This is excellent Moira comedy. The joke is that she treated her own salary negotiation as a performance — she added the bracelet demand not because she wanted it but because it played better dramatically. The two-word punchline 'Dramatic effect. Symbolism.' is perfectly economical and deeply character-specific. Only Moira Rose would perform for an audience of one in a private business negotiation.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, it's not a commercial, it's a radio spot. There's a distinction.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Radio is actually a very intimate medium. It's just me and the listener, in their car, or their kitchen, or their... bathroom.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It's quite prestigious, actually. Very old Hollywood.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Oh, it doesn't matter who's starring!' — dismissing Nicole Kidman's involvement in a film she's describing
jokes.setup: Twyla asks if Nicole Kidman is in the film Moira turned down
jokes.punchline: Moira dismisses Kidman's star power as irrelevant — implying she considers herself the more important figure in the story
jokes.explanation: Moira's self-aggrandizement is reliable comedy. The joke works because dismissing Nicole Kidman is objectively absurd, but Moira says it with complete sincerity. Solid character beat.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I am a former actress and philanthropist.

jokes.text: Twyla: 'I feel like I've been waiting on a famous person.' Moira: 'Twyla, you're not waiting on a famous person. She's already arrived!'
jokes.setup: Twyla says she feels like she's been serving a famous person lately
jokes.punchline: Moira corrects her — not to deny fame, but to say fame has 'already arrived,' i.e., it's a present-tense fact not a feeling
jokes.explanation: Clean setup/punchline with excellent Moira character work. The correction is technically true but also wildly self-aggrandizing. The rhythm of the exchange is crisp and the punchline lands with good comic timing implied by the structure.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I was just about to hear some very important business news, but apparently my sandwich is more important.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Look at this beauty. Medium rare roast beef, Swiss cheese, a little horseradish. She's a real looker.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I've been faithful to this sandwich. We've had some good times together. And now she's getting cold on me.

jokes.text: Roland: 'Johnny, this better be good. I got a hot little BLT over at the café and I don't wanna keep her waitin'.'
jokes.setup: Johnny calls Roland and Stevie into a private meeting with urgency
jokes.punchline: Roland arrives complaining that he's left behind a sandwich — personifying it as a 'her' — making his priorities comically misaligned with the business context
jokes.explanation: Calling the BLT 'her' and saying he doesn't want to keep 'her' waiting is a perfect Roland move — both absurd and completely in character. His priorities are always slightly wrong.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, I have some news. There's been some interest in the business.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Interest? From who?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, I don't want to get ahead of myself, but let's just say it's someone very interested in what we're doing.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Johnny, who is it?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: It's... well, it's a feeling, really. A very strong feeling that someone might be interested.

jokes.text: Roland: 'Okay. Yeah, I don't know about me. I mean, if the local papers ask me about the rumour that you might be getting a phone call...'
jokes.setup: Johnny asks them to keep the news secret so the family doesn't get their hopes up
jokes.punchline: Stevie says she can keep it secret. Roland immediately imagines a scenario where the 'local papers' are asking him about the rumor of a phone call — catastrophically overthinking his own inability to keep secrets
jokes.explanation: Roland's self-aware inability to keep secrets, expressed through the absurd scenario of 'local papers' asking about a phone call rumor, is good Roland comedy. The joke is in the specificity of his anxiety and his own acknowledgment of his weakness.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah, I'm great at keeping secrets. I haven't told anyone that you're bald.

jokes.text: Roland: 'you should check to make sure this place isn't bugged. We wouldn't want the big news to leak out! It is big news... probably.'
jokes.setup: Johnny dismisses Roland after the secret-keeping conversation
jokes.punchline: Roland's dramatic 'check for bugs' warning, followed by his complete undermining of his own statement with 'It is big news... probably' — a self-deflating button
jokes.explanation: The '...probably' is the joke — Roland can't even commit to hyping the news he was just excited about. Functional Roland character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Oh, an investor? Well, I won't say a word. Your secret's safe with me. Though I gotta say, if word got out that there's some mysterious money man sniffing around the town, people might start asking questions. They might wonder what you're hiding. Before you know it, there's rumors. Wild rumors. Maybe you're selling the town? Maybe you're building a casino? Maybe you're both!

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Yeah, I started getting dressed for work and I got distracted.' Johnny: 'What could have possibly distracted you enough to do that to yourself?'
jokes.setup: Alexis explains she got distracted while getting dressed
jokes.punchline: Johnny's 'do that to yourself' treats the sweatpants as a form of self-harm — the Rose family's relationship to casual clothing is comedically pathological
jokes.explanation: The Roses' horror at casual clothing is a well-established character trait, and Johnny's framing of sweatpants as something done TO herself plays nicely on that. Good character integration.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Are those... sweatpants?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Yes, they're so comfortable.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Alexis, I'm genuinely concerned for your wellbeing. This is a cry for help.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'This weird little desert daisy thing—' David: 'It's a succulent.'
jokes.setup: Alexis describes the dead plant Ted gave her
jokes.punchline: David's reflexive correction of 'desert daisy' to 'succulent' — even in an emotional conversation he can't resist the precise nomenclature correction
jokes.explanation: Classic David pedantry in an emotional moment. The joke is in the rhythm — Alexis is vulnerable and David just can't help but correct 'desert daisy.' Economy is excellent (two words to land the joke).

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: It died.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, we can resurrect it. I mean, I'm assuming you're talking about your style, which, let's be honest, has been on life support for quite some time.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Literally not supposed to die, and yet it did. Like what does that say about me?' David: 'I think that's a longer conversation.'
jokes.setup: Alexis spirals into existential despair about what killing an 'unkillable' succulent means about her
jokes.punchline: David's 'that's a longer conversation' — agreeing that there IS something significant here but declining to get into it. It's simultaneously caring and devastating.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best jokes because it works on multiple levels. David's acknowledgment that the question DOES say something about Alexis (by saying 'that's a longer conversation' rather than 'nothing') is funnier and more cutting than a direct insult would be. It's economical, character-specific, and has real emotional undertone that gives it rewatch value. David's growth means he doesn't pile on — he just gently defers.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: This plant was the only thing I had left from our relationship.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, you also have memories.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Yes, David, but I can't put memories in a pot and water them.

jokes.text: David: 'I never thought I'd have to say this, but there is only space in this family for one unstable sibling, and I have held that title for a very long time, so you are going to have to get it together.'
jokes.setup: Alexis is spiraling and David is trying to encourage her to come to his bachelor party
jokes.punchline: David frames his concern for Alexis entirely in terms of sibling competition — his 'intervention' is a territorial claim on the identity of 'unstable sibling'
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's strongest joke. The structure is perfect — David frames genuine sibling care as competitive brand protection. 'I have held that title for a very long time' is both self-aware (David knows his own history) and absurd (it's a terrible title to compete for). The joke earns its length because each clause builds. It's quotable, character-defining, and has the warm-but-sharp tone Schitt's Creek does at its best. High quotability.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Excuse me, I'm sorry, but I have been the family mess for quite some time now, and I don't appreciate you just waltzing in here and trying to take over my thing.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: David, you're not the only one allowed to have problems.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, actually, in this family, yes. Yes, I am. That's kind of my brand.

jokes.text: David suggests Alexis take 'a couple of Mom's happy pills' and put the situation 'on ice until tomorrow'
jokes.setup: David is trying to solve Alexis's emotional crisis so she'll come to his bachelor party
jokes.punchline: His solution is pharmaceutical — borrowing Moira's pills — and his framing of putting the whole emotional situation 'on ice' is comically cavalier
jokes.explanation: Moira's 'happy pills' is an established Rose family fact, so this lands as character comedy. David's dismissal of emotional processing in favor of chemical suppression is both funny and consistent with his self-preservation instincts.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I was at a private screening with Jo Foster just last week, and she was telling me about a very similar situation.

jokes.text: Moira asks if she can skip the escape room because 'Jo Foster once screened [Panic Room] for me privately and I would like to keep that memory safe.'
jokes.setup: Moira wants to get out of the escape room event, using Panic Room as an excuse
jokes.punchline: She invokes a private screening with 'Jo Foster' as her reason — name-dropping casually while also confusing the event (it's an escape room, not a movie screening), and protecting the 'memory' of a film as though it's sacred
jokes.explanation: This callbacks the Panic Room confusion from earlier while also being a great Moira moment — she's still confused about what an escape room is, she name-drops Jodie Foster casually, and she treats a film screening as a precious personal memory to protect. The rewatch bonus is for how it connects to the earlier joke.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, actually, I didn't have a bachelor party. I had a bachelor evening where I sat in a chair while four men I barely know stood around me in complete silence.

jokes.text: David: 'It's an escape room followed by a really sad reception, and everyone will be participating. Thank you so much!'
jokes.setup: Moira tries to get out of the escape room, David is forcing everyone to come
jokes.punchline: David describes his own bachelor party as 'a really sad reception' — confirming his own low expectations — while delivering mandatory participation with cheerful finality
jokes.explanation: David calling his own party 'really sad' is a funny bit of self-awareness. The 'Thank you so much!' button with its fake cheerfulness is a good cap.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: His name is Mike, not Ike.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, that's what I'm saying. Mike's been calling all morning, and now Ike's calling too.

jokes.text: Johnny tries to move the bachelor party to the motel so he doesn't miss the investor call; Stevie's reaction is visible resignation
jokes.setup: Johnny suggests the party be moved to the motel — ostensibly convenient for 'games'
jokes.punchline: Stevie's reaction to the transparent excuse, and Johnny's absurd suggestion they could 'escape from one room to the other'
jokes.explanation: The 'escape from one room to the other' line is a good absurdist image that grounds the scene. Functional plot-comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: It's not Mike Morrison, it's just Mike.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: No, no, no, it's Mike Morrison. I took a call from Mike Morrison.

jokes.text: Roland says he'll 'come running' when 'Ike Morrison' calls — revealing he misheard the name Mike as Ike
jokes.setup: Johnny told Roland to man the phones for an important call from Mike Morrison
jokes.punchline: Roland says 'Ike Morrison' — setting up the reveal that he literally doesn't know the name of the person he's waiting for a call from, which becomes plot-critical
jokes.explanation: This is a well-constructed setup — Roland's 'Ike' misidentification is planted here and pays off immediately when it turns out Mike already called. The structure is tight and the comedy has narrative stakes, which elevates it. Rewatch bonus because on rewatch you catch the foreshadowing.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I've been trying to reach you all day. Your business partner called.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: My business partner? Which one?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I don't know, some guy. He said it was urgent.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Oh my God. Oh my God, no. That was... that was the investor call. How did you not tell me?

jokes.text: Roland reveals Mike Morrison already called — while Johnny was right there — because Roland heard 'Ike' and didn't connect it to the person Johnny was waiting for
jokes.setup: Roland misheard 'Mike' as 'Ike' when Johnny mentioned the investor's name
jokes.punchline: A Mike Morrison actually called and Roland passed it off — the miscommunication had real consequences, and the reveal is perfectly timed
jokes.explanation: The payoff of the Ike/Mike setup is excellent. Roland's incompetence has real stakes, and the revelation unfolds through dialogue in a well-paced way. Stevie's aside 'maybe this is why Roland shouldn't be in charge of phones' is the perfect button. Strong structure.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Hi, this is John Rose calling. I'm calling about... I'm calling to discuss a business matter. A matter of business. That's what this call is regarding. Business. I'll call back if I don't hear from you. Or you can call me back. Either way, someone will be calling someone.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'maybe this is why Roland shouldn't be in charge of phones'
jokes.setup: It's revealed Roland missed the important call because he misheard the name
jokes.punchline: Stevie's perfectly understated 'maybe this is why' — the mildest possible response to a fairly catastrophic communication failure
jokes.explanation: Perfect Stevie deadpan button. The understatement is the joke — she treats a professional disaster with the mild disapproval of a bad coffee order.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: How did my voicemail sound?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: It sounded fine.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Except you didn't leave one.

jokes.text: Johnny's rambling voicemail to Mike Morrison, saying his own name three times and fumbling through: 'Sorry I didn't get your message. Uh, a few technical difficulties on our end... Uh, Johnny Rose calling. Uh, Johnny Rose calling you back. Uh, bye.'
jokes.setup: Johnny gets Mike Morrison's voicemail and has to leave a message under pressure
jokes.punchline: The message is a rambling disaster — repeating his name three times, vague about 'technical difficulties,' and ending with a flat 'bye'
jokes.explanation: The voicemail cringe is well-executed. Johnny saying his own name three times is both realistic (people do this) and escalatingly funny. The 'technical difficulties' euphemism for Roland's incompetence is a nice touch. The final 'bye' is a perfect deflating button.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I can assure you that my performance will be anything but flat.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'Is there an option to redo it?' — after Johnny's disastrous voicemail
jokes.setup: Johnny has just left a rambling, embarrassing voicemail
jokes.punchline: Stevie immediately asks if there's a redo option — implying the message was unsalvageable
jokes.explanation: Another Stevie deadpan button. Her question is functionally comic because the answer is clearly no and she knows it — it's an expression of despair disguised as a practical question. Short and effective.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: This is... this is truly extraordinary. And by extraordinary, I mean a crime against the English language and good taste. 'Larry Air: We get you there.' We get you there? Where? To bankruptcy? To a state of profound disappointment? The alliteration is non-existent, the messaging is vapid, and the tagline sounds like something a sleep-deprived intern would scribble on a napkin at 3 AM. This is advertising's nadir.

jokes.text: Moira to the radio producer: 'Oh wow, a prolific amount of material. And I am being paid for each spot, so hah!'
jokes.setup: Moira sees the stack of copy she needs to record for the airline ad
jokes.punchline: She uses 'prolific' incorrectly (it means producing much, not receiving much) and immediately pivots to a celebratory assumption she's being paid per spot — which is immediately corrected
jokes.explanation: Two-part Moira joke: she uses 'prolific' with misplaced confidence, then her financial assumption is immediately crushed. The 'hah!' of triumph followed by instant deflation is well-timed.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, I once dated a guy named Dusty who lived in a van, and I thought that was rock bottom. But then I realized the van didn't have a bathroom, so I was literally living in a porta-potty for three months.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: My God.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: So yes, Mom, a radio job is definitely not my lowest point.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Con brio, si!' — responding to the producer's instruction for high energy
jokes.setup: The producer asks for 'high energy' on the recording
jokes.punchline: Moira responds in Italian musical terminology ('con brio' = with vigor/spirit), treating a regional airline radio ad as a concert performance
jokes.explanation: Moira's theatrical self-inflation in a humble context is a reliable and well-executed joke. 'Con brio, si!' treats a radio spot as though it's La Scala. Very Moira.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yes, well, I'm just checking something very important on my phone.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Dad, it's your son's bachelor party.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I know that, David. But the market is in freefall, and I need to—

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: John, put the phone away.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You don't understand. This is a critical moment in— Oh my God, it's worse than I thought.

jokes.text: Moira reads 'We really care... at Larry Air.' and says: 'I like that, it's snappy.'
jokes.setup: Moira begins reading the airline copy — which is objectively terrible advertising writing
jokes.punchline: She genuinely compliments the weakest possible tagline with 'I like that, it's snappy' — her standards have been so thoroughly lowered by this gig that bad rhyme-scheme copy reads as quality to her
jokes.explanation: A gentle joke about bad advertising copy and Moira's misplaced enthusiasm. The irony is that she's more enthusiastic about the weakest line. Functional but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: No. We're doing the escape room.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Oh, that's a touch uninspired.' — reacting to 'Never flown Larry Air? Try Larry Air.' then 'That's a bit chummy for an airline.' — reacting to 'Get your derriere on Larry Air.'
jokes.setup: Moira is reading increasingly terrible airline advertising copy
jokes.punchline: Her running editorial commentary — 'uninspired,' 'a bit chummy' — treats terrible corporate copy as serious artistic critique, escalating with each new piece of bad writing
jokes.explanation: The escalating critique works well — 'a bit chummy for an airline' is particularly good because it implies Moira has strong views on the appropriate tone-register for airline advertising. The escalation drives the scene comedically.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I don't think this is a good idea. Escape rooms are designed for people who enjoy being trapped in confined spaces with strangers while being timed. I enjoy none of those things.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: It'll be fun, David. It's team building.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I would rather be actually trapped. In an actual room. With an actual lock. Alone.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Larry Air. We'll take you from there... to here...' — then: 'Now that should be switched, shouldn't it? You should—' breaking down mid-recording
jokes.setup: Moira is recording increasingly absurd copy and her patience is eroding
jokes.punchline: She can't stop herself from noting the logical flaw mid-take — 'from there to here' is directionally backwards — and she breaks out of the performance to editorialize
jokes.explanation: The 'there to here' observation is genuinely funny because it IS a dumb mistake in the copy, and Moira can't maintain her professionalism in the face of it. The cringe escalates with each line she tries to deliver.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: He can't even say 'fun' without sarcasm.

jokes.text: Alexis to the recording booth: 'I'm so sorry. This is like the first offer I could get after that whole Sunrise Bay thing went down.'
jokes.setup: Moira is breaking down recording terrible airline copy and Alexis has to intervene
jokes.punchline: Alexis apologizes to the producer for her mother, revealing she booked this gig partly out of desperation, which makes the whole situation more humiliating
jokes.explanation: The 'Sunrise Bay' reference adds continuity and makes the humiliation more layered. Alexis's desperation as a publicist is both comedic and sympathetic. Functional scene work.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: The Rose family has this wonderful tradition where we all gather and share what we're grateful for.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: That's lovely. How long does that usually take?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Oh, it varies. Sometimes an hour, sometimes two.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Right. Well, I'm grateful for a lot of things, so I could probably wrap mine up in about thirty seconds if that helps.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Have I made a terrible mistake, Alexis? Is this the lowest point of my life?' Alexis: 'Okay, look who you're talking to. I haven't showered in like two days. Is this the lowest point of my life?'
jokes.setup: Moira is in despair about the quality of the gig she's doing
jokes.punchline: Alexis deflects Moira's existential question by pointing to her own current state — unwashed, in sweatpants — as a competing lowest point, which is both funny and grounds the episode's emotional thread
jokes.explanation: Good use of the episode's parallel storylines. Alexis's self-deprecating redirect to her own two-day no-shower is both funny and thematically connective. The structure mirrors the sibling-competition joke from earlier, but this one is warmer.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, so this is an escape room? Like, we have to escape?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God. We couldn't escape to the Galapagos, so now we have to escape from a room. That's actually insane.

jokes.text: The studio crew quietly noting they can hear the entire conversation between Moira and Alexis outside the booth
jokes.setup: Moira and Alexis are having a private emotional conversation about the humiliating gig
jokes.punchline: The crew has heard everything — including Moira's breakdown and Alexis's confession about desperation booking
jokes.explanation: A reliable cringe comedy beat — characters discovered to have been overheard. Not particularly original but well-timed and adds to the mounting embarrassment of the scene.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, the Galapagos Islands? I literally just told you about my trip there!

jokes.text: Alexis tells Moira to start the car and she'll cover for her — then records Moira's parts herself: 'Try one of our in-flight meals, if you dare... at Larry Air.'
jokes.setup: Alexis sends Moira to escape while she stays to handle the recording
jokes.punchline: Alexis records the line herself, adding an unauthorized 'if you dare' in the middle — making the already-bad airline copy actively threatening
jokes.explanation: 'If you dare... at Larry Air' is a genuinely funny improvised addition because it makes the airline sound dangerous and aggressive — the opposite of what any airline wants. The absurdity of Alexis's 'fix' being worse than the original is a great joke. Quotable phrase.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Can you just show us where the exit is?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: David, we don't break character. That's Acting 101.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You're destroying the fourth wall, and quite frankly, the immersion.

jokes.text: Patrick's family's bachelor party tradition is revealed — 'annual escape room challenge has been a Brewer family tradition' — met with the Roses' visible unenthusiasm
jokes.setup: Patrick explains enthusiastically why he wanted an escape room for his bachelor party
jokes.punchline: The Rose family's barely-concealed horror at the concept of an 'annual family tradition' involving puzzles contrasts with Patrick's genuine excitement
jokes.explanation: The emotional subtext (Patrick missing his family, sharing the tradition with his new one) gives this warmth, and the comedy comes from the Roses being the wrong people to share this tradition with. The contrast is the joke.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I don't like mind games. They're manipulative, they're dishonest, and frankly, I'm not very good at them.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'Have they passed?' — after Patrick mentions his family couldn't be there for the party
jokes.setup: Patrick says his family couldn't be there, sharing the tradition with the Roses instead
jokes.punchline: Johnny immediately wonders if Patrick's family has died — a wildly dark interpretation of 'couldn't be here' that the situation clearly doesn't support
jokes.explanation: Johnny's 'Have they passed?' is a well-executed dark joke — his social cluelessness expressed through an absurdly morbid interpretation. It's also a character-specific joke: Johnny is always slightly out of step socially. Efficient and lands well.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Maybe I made a mistake.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: The outfit? Yes. Life choices? We can't know that yet.

jokes.text: David: 'What?!' — reacting to the Galapagos/Darwin theme of the escape room
jokes.setup: The game voice reveals the escape room is Galapagos-themed, involving Darwin and finches
jokes.punchline: David's reaction beat — his horror at a theme that directly references the Galapagos trip he didn't take — is the joke. He can't escape the reminder of his life choices.
jokes.explanation: The cosmic joke of the Galapagos escape room when Alexis is dealing with Ted and the Galapagos subtext is well-deployed. David's reaction beat sells the irony.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god, that's a finch!

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: How do you know that?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I dated a birdwatcher in the Maldives. Well, I thought he was a birdwatcher. Turns out he was just following me around with binoculars.

jokes.text: Moira immediately tries to get Darwin's assistant to show them the exit on the grounds that she's 'in the Guild'
jokes.setup: The escape room game voice mentions there's an assistant character who 'sides with Darwin'
jokes.punchline: Moira immediately attempts to leverage a fictional character's union membership to skip the entire game
jokes.explanation: Exceptional Moira joke. She tries to invoke the actors' union (the Guild) to get a shortcut from a fictional character in a game — treating a game actor as a professional peer with union obligations. The logic is internally consistent within Moira's world while being completely absurd. 'Don't worry, I'm also in the Guild' is immediately one of the funniest things she's said. High originality, high character integration. Comparable to the 8.0 anchor.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Where do you see yourself in five years?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Um, I don't know. Somewhere fabulous, hopefully. Like, maybe I'll have my own business, or I'll be married to someone really rich and successful. Or, like, both. Why are you asking me this?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm just trying to understand what your goals are.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Okay, well, can we not do this right now? I'm actually in the middle of this puzzle, and I'm really close to finishing it, so...

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's a 500-piece puzzle, Alexis.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I know, and I'm almost done. I just need to find this one piece, and then I can, like, really think about my future and my career and all of that stuff.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Don't worry, I'm also in the Guild.' — to the escape room's fictional assistant character
jokes.setup: Moira is trying to get the game's fictional assistant to help them escape immediately
jokes.punchline: She invokes being 'in the Guild' (presumably ACTRA/SAG, the actors' union) as leverage with a fictional game character — implying she thinks professional actor solidarity applies here
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The specificity of 'I'm also in the Guild' as a way to bond with a fictional character is peak Moira absurdism — she's so thoroughly an actor that she extends professional actor courtesy to characters inside an escape room game. It's original, deeply character-specific, and the economy is perfect (five words). Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Alexis, you're doing it wrong.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm doing it wrong? You're the one who said you were good at puzzles!

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I am good at puzzles. I'm not good at watching you destroy them.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, maybe if you weren't hovering over me like some kind of puzzle police, I could actually concentrate.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: The puzzle police? Alexis, we have approximately four minutes before this mechanism resets.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Then stop talking and let me work!

jokes.text: Johnny: 'I have a compass on my phone. Uh, when do we get our phones back?'
jokes.setup: The game involves directional clues about finches — east to west
jokes.punchline: Johnny's immediate pivot to phone-based solutions for a game designed to work without phones, and his follow-up about when they get phones back — seeding the phone obsession that pays off throughout the episode
jokes.explanation: Establishes the phone running gag that drives the escape room act. Johnny's solution to everything being technology-based is consistent with his character as an old-school businessman trying to stay modern.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: The game is fun!

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh really? Because last week you said it was 'a waste of time and brain cells.' What changed? Oh, I know—the guy running it is attractive.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: That's not true.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Alexis, you once said our family was 'boring and depressing' until you found out Johnny Rose was wealthy.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Oh, I used to share your philosophic optimism, dear, but know the reality is, we're never getting out of here.'
jokes.setup: Patrick tries to encourage Moira that they can escape the room
jokes.punchline: Moira's fatalistic 'we're never getting out of here' — delivered with resigned certainty — treats an escape room as a philosophical existential trap rather than a solvable puzzle
jokes.explanation: Moira's existential despair about an escape room is wonderfully character-specific — she's not bad at puzzles, she's just philosophically given up. 'We're never getting out of here' lands as both funny and somehow poignant.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Has anyone seen my phone? I'm expecting a very important call.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Darling, you know the rules. No phones during family time.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mom's right. We all agreed—

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah, no phones.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: This is different. This could be the call that changes everything.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: And it will still change everything in twenty minutes.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Besides, I don't even have mine on me.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Nor I.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Same.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Really? You're all completely phone-free right now?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Completely.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Utterly.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: *phone buzzes*

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: *phone buzzes*

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: *phone buzzes*

jokes.text: Stevie finds the first finch: 'Just found a finch. The first number is six, people!'
jokes.setup: Everyone is struggling with the puzzle and complaining
jokes.punchline: Stevie — the one who planned this and seemed most resigned — immediately solves the first puzzle while everyone else whines, with cheerful competence
jokes.explanation: Stevie's competence while everyone complains is a nice character beat. Her 'people!' is a funny energy shift. Functional but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: A candle.

jokes.text: David: 'Okay, see this is why I don't like mind games. It puts you in a situation where you're made to feel dumb, even though you're not. Like, some people's minds just don't work like this.'
jokes.setup: David is struggling with the escape room puzzles
jokes.punchline: His philosophical justification for why he's bad at puzzles — framing it as a systemic injustice rather than personal failure — is both self-aware and self-serving
jokes.explanation: David's defensive justification for not being good at puzzles is consistent with his character — he'd rather reframe the problem than accept failure. 'Some people's minds just don't work like this' is a good line.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, so you got that symbol tattooed on you because...?

dialogue.speaker: Friend
dialogue.line: It's an ancient Egyptian hieroglyph! It means 'eternal life' or something.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Or something? You put 'or something' permanently on your body?

dialogue.speaker: Friend
dialogue.line: Well, that's what the guy at the tattoo parlor said.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: The guy at the tattoo parlor. Right. Because that's where I would go for Egyptology expertise.

jokes.text: Alexis finds a finch by spotting it from across the room: 'No, the finch. Second row, in the middle. Trust me, Stavros and I used to make out in his parents' aviary.'
jokes.setup: Alexis says 'there' and David asks where — seeming to point at something else
jokes.punchline: She found the finch because of her background making out in an aviary — the most Alexis-Rose explanation possible for a useful skill
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best jokes. The specificity of 'Stavros and I used to make out in his parents' aviary' is exactly the kind of absurd-but-plausible Alexis backstory that makes the character. The fact that it's useful in the escape room is perfect comic logic — her ridiculous past life has practical applications. Highly quotable and deeply character-integrated. Would get a big laugh.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Play the next one.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: This is my voicemail. I left myself a message.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Why would you do that?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Because I wanted to remember that everyone wanted to bail on the company.

jokes.text: David: 'Okay, there's no key in here. There is no key in here.' — Patrick: 'That's just the first puzzle, David. It's a series of puzzles.'
jokes.setup: The team has cracked the journal code and David opens it triumphantly
jokes.punchline: No key. David's crisis at discovering the escape room has multiple stages reveals his assumption that one puzzle would end the whole thing
jokes.explanation: David's shock and disappointment that escape rooms have multiple puzzles is a good character beat — his impatience and expectation of immediate results are perfectly captured.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So Alexis is the key to solving the escape room. The irony being that she couldn't figure out how to leave a country.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Wait, wait, everyone! I hear my phone ringing. That's the phone I was talking about. So if I could just leave the lab for one minute...' Game voice announces: 'You have 52 minutes to escape.'
jokes.setup: The whole group is obsessed with getting their phones — Johnny for the investor call, Moira apparently for her own reasons
jokes.punchline: Moira tries to leave mid-game to get her phone, is denied by the game clock announcement — the absurdity of the game-voice timing is perfect
jokes.explanation: Good use of the game's mechanical voice as a comedic timing device. The '52 minutes!' response from David is the right button.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What does this mean? What are you saying?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm saying that I thrive under pressure. I'm saying that chaos is my natural element.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You thrive under pressure? Alexis, you once cried because we ran out of oat milk.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: That was different.

jokes.text: David: 'Oh my God, this game is actually fun.' Patrick: 'See? I told you!' David: 'You're only saying that because you got one right!'
jokes.setup: David has just succeeded at a puzzle and is now enjoying himself
jokes.punchline: He admits the game is fun, but immediately deflects Patrick's 'I told you so' by accusing Patrick of only feeling validated because of success — projecting his own motivations onto Patrick
jokes.explanation: David's logic — Patrick is only saying it's fun because he got one right — is pure projection and completely wrong, which is the joke. His defensive reflex even when enjoying himself is perfectly David.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Maybe it died because it outgrew its pot. Like, metaphorically.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It died because you didn't water it.

jokes.text: Johnny's big investor revelation in the middle of the escape room — the family's collective reaction of 'Why didn't you say this before?! Get the phone!' followed by revelation everyone kept their phones
jokes.setup: Johnny has been desperately trying to get his phone all episode for the investor call
jokes.punchline: When he finally reveals the reason, the family offers phones immediately — and it turns out they ALL still have their phones (phones weren't confiscated from everyone). Johnny was the only one without.
jokes.explanation: The payoff that everyone has their phones except Johnny — the one person who needed his — is a well-constructed escalation and reversal. The structure of the running gag pays off with a twist that retroactively makes the whole phone obsession funnier. Good narrative comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm like this plant. I've outgrown my pot and I need a bigger one.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, well, the symbolism is not lost on me.

jokes.text: David solves the candle riddle instantly: 'Candle.' — after the riddle is read
jokes.setup: 'I'm tall when I'm young and short when I'm old.' — Patrick reads a riddle
jokes.punchline: David immediately answers 'Candle.' with no fanfare — someone who claimed to hate mind games just solved one instantly
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in the contrast — David complained that his mind doesn't work like this, and then immediately solves a riddle. One word, maximum impact. Economy perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm like this plant. I'm wilting. I need care and attention.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, I understand the metaphor, Alexis. But if you could just tell me directly what you need, that would be great.

jokes.text: David: 'Okay, this is an ancient Egyptian symbol for stability. My friend Prairie got one tattooed on her lower-back in seventh grade.'
jokes.setup: The laser reveals an ancient Egyptian symbol and David needs to identify what it means for the puzzle
jokes.punchline: David's knowledge of Egyptian hieroglyphs comes from a friend's tramp stamp — an absurd provenance for useful archaeological knowledge, delivered as though it's perfectly normal
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The specificity — 'Prairie,' 'lower-back,' 'seventh grade' — makes this brilliantly absurd. The joke works because the provenance of the knowledge (a friend's tramp stamp at age 12) is the least scholarly possible source for Egyptological information, but David deploys it with complete authority. Deeply character-specific and quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: My husband's business acumen has been nothing short of perspicacious. His entrepreneurial endeavors have demonstrated a rather pellucid understanding of commercial machinations, and I must say, his fiscal prognostications have proven remarkably prescient. It's all very... quotidian, really.

jokes.text: Alexis identifies the Egyptian symbol because her friend Prairie got one tattooed on her lower back in seventh grade — 'My friend Prairie got one tattooed on her lower-back in seventh grade'
jokes.setup: They've identified an ancient Egyptian symbol for stability and need to know what it means
jokes.punchline: Alexis knows it because of a seventh-grade friend's tramp stamp — her knowledge base is entirely composed of chaotic personal history
jokes.explanation: Excellent Alexis joke. The specificity — seventh grade, Prairie, lower-back tattoo — is perfect. It's absurd (who has a tramp stamp in seventh grade?) while being entirely believable in Alexis's world. It deepens her escape room competence joke: she's solving everything through the most chaotic knowledge base possible. High character integration and quotability.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I just want to say that this moment is truly special, and I feel compelled to share some thoughts about the journey we've all been on together, the bonds we've formed, and the way life has a funny way of—

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: We specifically asked for no speeches.

jokes.text: David: '...So like mummies, Rami Malek, a... a pyramid.' — listing things from Egypt
jokes.setup: After identifying the Egyptian stability symbol, David says they need to find something from Egypt
jokes.punchline: His list of Egyptian things includes 'Rami Malek' — a contemporary actor listed alongside mummies and pyramids as a representative cultural artifact of ancient Egypt
jokes.explanation: Rami Malek sandwiched between mummies and pyramids as David's understanding of 'Egyptian things' is a funny character joke — his cultural knowledge is filtered entirely through contemporary Hollywood. The pause before 'a pyramid' adds good rhythm.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'd like to say a few words...

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Thanks! I just like wanted to get to the drinks.' — explaining why she solved the puzzle so effectively
jokes.setup: Patrick calls Alexis's puzzle-solving 'actually pretty impressive'
jokes.punchline: Alexis attributes her problem-solving to wanting alcohol — making her motivation the antithesis of intellectual engagement, which somehow also feels like a compliment to her priorities
jokes.explanation: Good character button — Alexis's most impressive moment is entirely motivated by wanting a drink. It also has a warmth to it because it worked. Her 'like' usage is authentically Alexis.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Okay, I think we should probably start wrapping this up. We have an early morning tomorrow.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Wait, let me see those photos. These are terrible. This one is completely washed out, and this angle is deeply unflattering. We need to retake them.

jokes.text: The voicemail playback reveals Moira left a message trying to escape the escape room: 'John, call me when you can. We need an excuse to get out of the escape room thing.'
jokes.setup: Johnny plays his voicemails on speaker for the family — expecting the investor call
jokes.punchline: The first voicemail is Moira trying to conspire to get out of the escape room — she left this message while inside the escape room, and everyone can hear it
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback payoff. Moira left the conspiracy message to Johnny while inside the escape room — which means she called him while they were in the same room. The structure of hearing it on speaker with the whole group present is perfectly humiliating. It also calls back the email/no-response joke from earlier — Moira's answer to problems is always phone calls. Strong rewatch bonus.

jokes.text: David's voicemail plays: 'Hi, just reminding you that you can't bail on the escape room thing, even though all of us want...' Johnny: 'Yeah, I don't think we need to keep...' Moira: 'I actually wanna hear that one.'
jokes.setup: The group is listening to voicemails and David's message starts playing
jokes.punchline: David's message confirms what Moira's message implied — everyone wanted to bail — but Moira wants to hear the full extent of David's phone-policing, despite being guilty of the same
jokes.explanation: The double irony — David sent enforcement messages while everyone (including him) wanted to bail — and Moira wanting to hear it for the receipts is sharp. Good comedic structure using the voicemail playback mechanic.

jokes.text: Mike Morrison's voicemail confirming he wants to take a meeting — played on speaker for the whole family who erupts in celebration
jokes.setup: The whole episode has built toward this call — Johnny has been desperate to get his phone back
jokes.punchline: The call comes through as voicemail, played on speaker in the middle of the escape room parking lot, and the family's collective eruption (after having no idea this was coming) is the comedic/emotional payoff
jokes.explanation: The episode's emotional climax working as comedy — the payoff is earned because of how thoroughly the setup has been seeded. The whole family celebrating a business meeting in a strip mall parking lot is both funny and warm. The 'Why am I still holding this?' line from Johnny is a perfect button.

jokes.text: Alexis develops an extended succulent metaphor about needing 'bigger pots' — David: 'I was tracking the metaphor, but um... what're you saying?'
jokes.setup: Alexis has been using the dead succulent as a metaphor for her own stagnation
jokes.punchline: David confirms he understood the metaphor — and then asks what she's saying, implying understanding the metaphor doesn't make the actual meaning clear
jokes.explanation: David's 'I was tracking the metaphor, but...' is a well-constructed line — it acknowledges comprehension while still expressing confusion, and it implies the metaphor is doing more heavy lifting than the substance behind it. Very David in its precise parsing of language.

jokes.text: Moira's speech: 'Before we find ourselves ebrious with hooch and happiness...' using 'ebrious' (an archaic word for intoxicated)
jokes.setup: Moira begins a toast/speech at the bachelor party reception despite being told 'no speeches'
jokes.punchline: 'Ebrious with hooch and happiness' — Moira combines an obscure archaic word with extremely casual slang in the same phrase, her vocabulary existing in no recognizable register
jokes.explanation: 'Ebrious' (archaic for drunk) paired with 'hooch' (extremely casual slang for alcohol) in the same breath is peak Moira register-mixing. This is high character integration — only Moira exists in this linguistic space. The word itself is funny without being a made-up word, which makes it better.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'Well, we did say no speeches, so—' Johnny: 'Guess they listened.' — as Moira begins speechifying anyway
jokes.setup: The group presumably agreed to 'no speeches' for the bachelor party
jokes.punchline: As Moira launches into a formal speech, Stevie notes they said no speeches — Johnny's 'Guess they listened' confirms no one listened, least of all Moira
jokes.explanation: A functional irony joke — the 'no speeches' rule immediately violated by the person most likely to violate it. Johnny's deadpan 'Guess they listened' is a good button.

jokes.text: David: 'Well, I think we have forgotten why we're here. It is our bachelor party. Also, whoever chose those photos, they were not approved.'
jokes.setup: The whole episode has culminated in celebration, but David reasserts his authority as the bachelor
jokes.punchline: David's priority-ordering: reclaim the bachelor party focus, THEN immediately complain about unapproved photos — implying there are photos on display that David hasn't vetted
jokes.explanation: Perfect episode button. David's concern about the photos — presumably the big photo of himself mentioned earlier — is a callback to his controlling nature and a perfect final character note. 'They were not approved' as a final line shows both his growth (he's grateful) and his stasis (he's still controlling). Economy is good and the laugh is earned.

tmdb_overview: The Roses attend the Escape Room portion of David and Patrick's Bachelor Party, but their participation wanes the more their personal dilemmas come to a head.
insight: David's deadpan refusals escalate into absurdist chaos across 57 jokes in a tight 47 minutes.
